posts.name: Erlend @ntonsen
posts.cooked:
The heatwave is now coming to Burgundy and Germany.
Days of 36C (97F) in Chambolle-Musigny… Any thoughts? Hope springs eternal?

posts.name: Josh Grossman
posts.cooked: 2019 exceeded my expectations and had a similar heat wave at the same time. Still some time to go on the vintage.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked: Horrible in Portugal. Temperatures of around 40 degrees celsius in many cities, with terrible fires in the Palmela region (close to several wineries).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The heatwave is now coming to Burgundy and Germany.
Days of 36C (97F) in Chambolle-Musigny… Any thoughts? Hope springs eternal?

Normal July temperatures for Burgundy are 30-35C…

This July, we have had a heat spike last week, and another coming on Monday-Tuesday. But the forecast for the rest of the month is 30-31 by day, 17 or so at night. That’s actually cooler than 2019, and if it holds true, I’ll sign on the dotted line.

I feel reassured to know.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
The heatwave is now coming to Burgundy and Germany.
Days of 36C (97F) in Chambolle-Musigny… Any thoughts? Hope springs eternal?

Normal July temperatures for Burgundy are 30-35C…

This July, we have had a heat spike last week, and another coming on Monday-Tuesday. But the forecast for the rest of the month is 30-31 by day, 17 or so at night. That’s actually cooler than 2019, and if it holds true, I’ll sign on the dotted line.

How are the vines holding up? My concern would be with those vines affected by frost or hail damage . . .

Cheers

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
The heatwave is now coming to Burgundy and Germany.
Days of 36C (97F) in Chambolle-Musigny… Any thoughts? Hope springs eternal?

Vintages are much more complex than just daytime temperatures. You need to consider water tables, terroir, vine age, nighttime temperatures, and of course, vineyard management, etc. Plus, it is still quite early. A lot can happen one way or the other.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:

How are the vines holding up? My concern would be with those vines affected by frost or hail damage . . .

Cheers

Well.

Some sunburn on exposed clusters, but beyond that looking good.

FYI folks, the heatwave in Bordeaux is much more serious.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: Not just heat, but if it’s anything like where we are, there might be an issue with a lack of rain as well, plus a good few days of warm & strong winds.

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked: That’s impressive by any standard

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked: Let the Oregonians know when it hits 119!

posts.name: Edward H. Earles
posts.cooked: Epoch Times is reporting that there is considerable wildfire near Bordeaux, with 23,800 acres reported as being burned. Portugal is also hard-hit.

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked:
Epoch Times is reporting that there is considerable wildfire near Bordeaux, with 23,800 acres reported as being burned. Portugal is also hard-hit.

That sucks. Big time.

But, yeah, no climate change.
And let’s not forget the entire Po River valley.

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked:
It’s really bad all over Portugal, Spain, and France.

We’re not even into August yet and they’ll be looking at warm and dry, low humidity is the issue, for some time. Low humidity is the fire starter.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
Epoch Times is reporting that there is considerable wildfire near Bordeaux, with 23,800 acres reported as being burned. Portugal is also hard-hit.

I’m in Languedoc right now (just east of Narbonne) and we can see smoke from all the fires. Can’t smell smoke though.

We walked up to the top of a hill from which on a normal day you can see the sea as we were only a few miles away. I could tell there was water there but I couldn’t actually see it due to the haze. Could just barely make out the Pyrenees as well.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
BBC (31 minutes ago): Heatwave: Ferocious European heat heads north

It was 42C/107.6 in Nantes, in the northwest of France, at the mouth of the Loire.

Just south of the city of Bordeaux:

Gironde, a popular tourist region in the south-west, has been hit particularly badly, with firefighters battling to control blazes which have destroyed nearly 17,000 hectares (42,000 acres) of land since last Tuesday.

“The idea that comes into my head is, it’s a monster,” Jean-Luc Gleyze, the president of the Gironde region said of the fires.

“It’s a monster like an octopus, and it’s growing and growing and growing in the front, in the back, on both sides. Because of the temperature, because of the wind, because of the lack of water in the air… it’s a monster and it’s very difficult to fight against it.”

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Let the Oregonians know when it hits 119!

It’s getting close. The BBC just reported:

Temperatures in Portugal hit 47C on Thursday - a record for July. Most of the country has been placed under high fire danger by the national meteorological office IPMA.

47C = 116.6F
